coredata.dc:title: Thin film nanocomposite membranes incorporated with graphene quantum dots for high flux and antifouling property
coredata.dc:creator: Bi, Ran
Zhang, Qi
Zhang, Runnan
Su, Yanlei
Jiang, Zhongyi
coredata.dc:description: Thin film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes incorporated with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were fabricated with enhanced water permeability and antifouling property. Owing to the small size, stable dispersion and active functional groups, GQDs were embedded into polyamide (PA) layer during the interfacial polymerization of piperazine (PIP) and trimesoyl chloride (TMC) in a facile way. The surface chemical features and morphologies of the resultant TFN membranes were characterized by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), water contact angle, zeta potential, scanning electron microscope (SEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) measurements. The surface roughness of the TFN membranes decreased and the surface hydrophilicity of the TFN membranes enhanced with the increase of the GQDs content. According to nanofiltration (NF) experiments, the highest water flux of the TFN membranes reached up to 102.0 L/(m2h) under operation pressure of 0.2 MPa, which was nearly 6.8 times as much as that of the pristine PA membrane. The TFN membranes also exhibited excellent antifouling performance that the steady water flux of the as-fabricated membrane under harsh fouling condition was about 4 times as much as that of the pristine PA membrane. This study may present a useful attempt of using carbon materials in fabricating high flux and antifouling membranes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Thin film nanocomposite membrane
Interfacial polymerization
Graphene quantum dots
High flux
Antifouling

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membranes
Characterization of GQDs and the GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membranes
Characterization of the GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membranes
Water fluxes and separation performance of the GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membranes
Antifouling performance of the GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membranes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of the preparation process and separation performance of the GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membranes.
TEM (a) and particle size distribution (b) images of GQDs.
XPS spectra of the PIP-TMC NF membrane (0#) and the GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membrane (1#).

SEM (a) and AFM (b) images of the PIP-TMC NF membrane (0#) and the GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membranes (1#, 3# and 6#). All of samples for AFM were freeze-dried for 12 h and then tested in a scan size of 2 µm × 2 µm.
Water contact angles of the PIP-TMC NF membrane (0#) and the GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membranes with different GQDs contents.
Zeta potential of the PIP-TMC NF membrane (0#) and the GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membranes with different GQDs contents.

Water fluxes (a) and rejections to dye/salt (b) of the PIP-TMC NF membrane (0#) and the GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membranes with different GQDs contents under operation pressure of 0.2 MPa. Each above value was based on the average of two independent membranes fabricated under the same condition.
Pore size distributions of the PIP-TMC NF membrane (0#) and the GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membranes (3#, 6#).
Schematic diagram of water channels in biological membranes (a) and the GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membranes (b).

Time-dependent flux of the PIP-TMC NF membrane (0#) and the GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membrane (3#) during the filtration of BSA, HA, and emulsified oil solutions. All experiments were carried out under operation pressure of 0.2 MPa.
the GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membranes prepared at different monomer concentrations.
Comparison of common nanoparticles used for the fabrication of TFN membranes.

row.entry: Dispersed completely via sonication for 1 h
The hydrogen bonds between particles lead to consequent agglomeration.
Dispersed completely via Sonication for 4 h followed by 30 min stirring

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The NF performance of TFN membranes with different nanoparticles in this work and literatures.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: CS-MMT: Chitosan-Montmorillonite; mZIF: poly(Sodium 4-styrenesulfonate) (PSS) modified ZIF-8; G-CNTm: MWNT-intercalated graphene nanofiltration membrane; Arg: Arginine; NF: Nanofiltration.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: RB5: Reactive Black 5; RR49: Reactive Red 49; RB2: Reactive Blue 2; MO: Methyl Orange; CR: Congo Red; OG: Orange GII.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Antifouling indexes and steady water fluxes of 0# and 3# membranes during filtration of BSA, HA, and emulsified oil solutions. All experiments were carried out under 0.2 MPa.

row.entry: a Steady water flux (L m−2 h−1)

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The steady water flux was measured at operation pressure of 0.2 MPa.

head.ce:title: Thin film nanocomposite membranes incorporated with graphene quantum dots for high flux and antifouling property

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Green Chemical Technology, School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300072, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Green Chemical Technology, School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Tianjin University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Collaborative Innovation Center of Chemical Science and Engineering (Tianjin), Tianjin University, Tianjin 300072, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Collaborative Innovation Center of Chemical Science and Engineering (Tianjin), Tianjin University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Key Laboratory for Green Chemical Technology, School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300072, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Green Chemical Technology, School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Tianjin University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Thin film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes incorporated with graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were fabricated with enhanced water permeability and antifouling property. Owing to the small size, stable dispersion and active functional groups, GQDs were embedded into polyamide (PA) layer during the interfacial polymerization of piperazine (PIP) and trimesoyl chloride (TMC) in a facile way. The surface chemical features and morphologies of the resultant TFN membranes were characterized by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), water contact angle, zeta potential, scanning electron microscope (SEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) measurements. The surface roughness of the TFN membranes decreased and the surface hydrophilicity of the TFN membranes enhanced with the increase of the GQDs content. According to nanofiltration (NF) experiments, the highest water flux of the TFN membranes reached up to 102.0 L/(m2h) under operation pressure of 0.2 MPa, which was nearly 6.8 times as much as that of the pristine PA membrane. The TFN membranes also exhibited excellent antifouling performance that the steady water flux of the as-fabricated membrane under harsh fouling condition was about 4 times as much as that of the pristine PA membrane. This study may present a useful attempt of using carbon materials in fabricating high flux and antifouling membranes.

The GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membranes were fabricated by interfacial polymerization of piperazine (PIP) and trimesoyl chloride (TMC) on the support membranes with GQDs as aqueous additives. The as-prepared membranes exhibit efficient separation performance.
fx1

ce:section.ce:para: For the past few years, freshwater scarcity poses great threat to the sustainable development of human society. Membrane technologies, including nanofiltration (NF) and reverse osmosis (RO), have found increasing applications in water purification and reclamation [1]. Thin film composite (TFC) membranes consisting of an ultrathin polyamide (PA) layer, are the most widely used NF and RO membranes. However, due to the inherent limitations of conventional TFC membranes, the preparation of a TFC membrane with a high flux while maintaining a good rejection is still a challenging task [2].

In recent years, considerable efforts have been devoted to explore the advanced thin film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes [3]. It has been found that the water fluxes and antifouling properties of the TFN membranes improved remarkably through the appropriate introduction of nanoparticles into PA layers [4]. However, subject to the poor dispersibility and compatibility of some nanoparticles in PA layers, there still exists a large room for the elevation of the membrane performance [5]. Nowadays, carbon materials have been widely used in sensing [6], electromagnetic interference (EMI) shielding [7], photocatalysts [8] and functional materials [9], because of its superior properties of electronic, mechanical, optical and transparent nature [10]. In some studies, carbon materials such as carbon nanotubes (CNTs) and graphene oxide (GO) nanosheets, were incorporated into the PA layers during interfacial polymerization. The obtained TFN membranes integrated the advantages of both polymer materials and carbon materials. For instance, Chan et al. reported a novel TFN membrane embedded with zwitterion functionalized CNTs [11]. The pure water flux of the resultant zwitterion functionalized CNT/PA membrane was above 4-fold than that of the pristine PA membrane, meanwhile, the salt rejections were also slightly increased. They suggested the zwitterion functionalized CNTs imparted an additional transport passway to the TFN membrane by inserting nanochannels into the PA layer. Shen et al. introduced poly(methyl methacrylate) grafted multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWNTs) into a PA layer [12]. The as-prepared membrane exhibited an up to 62% increase in water flux compared with the pristine PA membrane. Bano et al. reported the TFN membranes embedded with GO had higher permeation fluxes but negligible changes in the salt rejections [13].

Very recently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), which share most of the excellent features of GO, have emerged in broad application fields such as catalysis, bioimaging, lighting, sensing, biological label, optoelectronic and energy [14]. A number of researchers found that GQDs in small sizes could be synthesized through a facile and controllable “bottom-up” method [15]. Generally speaking, nanoparticles with large size can increase surface roughness and result in faster membrane fouling rates [16]. In contrast, Lind et al. found that smaller nanoparticles produced greater permeability enhancements and speculated that the nanoparticle size may be considered as an additional parameter in designing TFN membranes [17]. Latest research by Li et al. revealed that incorporating a super hydrophilic nanometer carbon dots (CDs) with quantum size of 6.8 nm into a PA layer led to a higher surface hydrophilicity and thus a higher water flux [18]. Based on the above research progress, we conjectured that the synthetic GQDs with a smaller size (~2 nm) and higher crystallinity [19] may be utilized in fabricating TFN membranes with high water fluxes and antifouling properties.

In this study, TFN membranes incorporated with GQDs were fabricated via interfacial polymerization of piperazine (PIP) and trimesoyl chloride (TMC) with GQDs as aqueous additives. The influence of the GQDs addition on surface morphology, hydrophilicity and charge density was investigated in detail. Separation and antifouling performance of the resultant TFN membranes was evaluated by using NF experiments. This study may present a useful attempt to the fabrication of TFN membranes with high fluxes and antifouling properties towards high-efficient water treatment.

Poly(ether sulfone) (PES Ultrason E6020P with M w = 29,000 g/mol) was purchased from BASF Co. (Germany). TMC was purchased from Alfa Co. (USA). PIP, n-heptane, humic acid (HA), Poly(ethylene glycol) (PEG, M w = 2000 Da), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), citric acid (CA), Na2SO4, Orange GII (C16H10N2Na2O7S2, M w = 452 Da), Congo Red (C32H22N6Na2O6S2, Mw= 696 Da) and other reagents were purchased from Kewei Chemical Reagent Co. (Tianjin, China). Bovine serum albumin (BSA) was purchased from Institute of Hematology, Chinese Academy of Medical Science (Tianjin, China). Sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) was purchased from Jiangtian Chemical Technology Co. Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Oil was edible soybean oil. The used water was the deionized water prepared by a reverse osmosis system.

GQDs were synthesized by a “bottom-up” method via thermal decomposition of CA [15]. 2.0 g of CA was put into a crucible, heated to 200 ℃ in an electric oven and kept for 15 min. During thermal treatment process, the solid CA material was liquefied and the color was gradually turned to orange, indicating the formation of GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membranes
ce:section.ce:para: PES support membranes were prepared via non-solvent induced phase separation (NIPS). The as-prepared support membranes had a wet thickness of 250 µm and were kept in deionized water before use. The pure water flux of the support membranes was about 180 L/(m2h) under 0.1 MPa and the BSA (67 kDa) rejection of the support membranes was about 98%. A series of GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membranes were then fabricated by interfacial polymerization of PIP and TMC on the support membranes with GQDs as aqueous additives. According to our previous work, the concentrations of PIP were chosen as 0.1 wt% [20]. The schematic illustration for the preparation process was shown in Fig. 1. The support membranes were firstly immersed into the aqueous solution for 15 min, which contained 0.1 wt% PIP and a certain content of GQDs. After that, the membranes were taken out and removed the residual liquid. The membranes were subsequently dipped in the 0.1 wt% TMC/n-heptane solution for 2 min to form a PA layer by interfacial polymerization. Finally, the resultant TFN membranes were air-dried at temperature of 60 ℃ for 15 min in an oven for further polymerization and complete evaporation of n-heptane. Different GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membranes prepared in this study were listed in Table 1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of GQDs and the GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membranes
ce:section.ce:para: High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of GQDs was recorded with a JEM 2100F transmission electron microscope. GQDs were dispersed in water, then dropped on a copper mesh and dried for measurement. Attenuated total reflectance Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR–FTIR, Nicolet 560) was utilized to investigate the functional groups on the membrane surfaces. The FTIR absorption spectroscopy was obtained in the region of 4000–400 cm−1 with a resolution of 4 cm−1 for 64 scans.

The surface chemical compositions of the membranes were analyzed by the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) instrument (PHI-1600, Perkin-Elmer Co., USA) using Mg Kα (1253.6 eV) as radiation source. The morphology of the membrane surfaces was acquired by a field-emission scanning electronic microscopy system (SEM, Nova Nanosem 430, FEI Co., USA). Atomic force microscopy (AFM, Nanoscope IIIA AFM, Digital Instruments, Santa Barbara, CA, USA.) in contact mode was utilized to further detect the surface roughness of the membranes. All of samples were freeze-dried for 12 h before test. The root-mean-squared roughness (Rms) was measured at two locations in one sample to get an average value.

Water contact angles of the membrane surfaces were measured using a contact angle goniometer (JC2000C Contact Angle Meter, Powereach Co., Shanghai, China) at room temperature. Anton-Paar streaming potential analyzer (Anton Paar KG, Austria) was used for the investigation of the surface charges of the membranes. The measurements were carried out under pH 6.0 ± 0.2 with the samples immersed in 1.0 mM KCl solution at 25 ± 0.5 ℃. The resultant zeta potential was calculated using the Helmholtz–Smoluchowski equation.

The separation performance of the membranes, including the water fluxes and rejections to dye/salt, was evaluated by a dead-end stirred filtration cell equipped with a nitrogen gas cylinder and solution reservoir. The experiments were carried out under a low-pressure of 0.2 MPa, with an effective membrane area of 28.7 cm2. The water flux was measured after the pre-compaction of membranes at 0.25 MPa for 0.5 h to obtain a stable value. The pure water flux J
w (L/(m2 h)) was calculated by the following equation:
(1)
J
w
=
V
A∆T
where V (L) was the volume of permeated water, A (m2) was the membrane area and ΔT (h) was the permeation time.

The rejection performance was evaluated with aqueous dye solutions (0.1 g/L, pH = 7.0) of Orange GII, Congo red and a salt solution (0.1 g/L, pH = 7.0) of Na2SO4, respectively. The rejection ratios (R) were calculated by equation:
(2)
R
=
C
f
-
C
p
C
f
× 100 %

C
p (g/L) and C
f (g/L) were the concentrations of permeate and feed solutions. Salt concentrations were measured by electrical conductivity (DDS–11A, Shanghai Leichi Instrument Co., Shanghai, China). Dye concentrations were measured with an ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (Hitach UV–2800, Hitach Co., Japan).

In addition, molecular weight cut-off (MWCO), average effective pore size (
μ
p
) and pore size distribution of the membranes were characterized by solute rejection experiments. A serious molecular weight of PEG (200, 400, 600, 1000, 2000 and 4000 Da) were used as the solutes [21,22]. The experiments were carried out under a constant pressure of 0.2 MPa. The rejection ratios (
R
p

The antifouling ability of the membranes was evaluated using the following procedure. 1.0 g/L BSA, HA and emulsified oil solutions were used as model foulants. BSA and HA were directly dissolved in ultrapure water (pH 6.0 ± 0.2). The emulsified oil solution was prepared by 0.9 g/L soybean oil and 0.1 g/L SDS mixed under high-speed stirring. The stabilized pure water flux (J
w1 ) of membranes was firstly obtained during NF with a constant pressure of 0.2 MPa. The filtrating feed solution was then switched to the foulant solution and the flux (J
F ) was measured continuously for 12 h. Finally, pure water flux (J
w2 ) was measured again after pure water cleaning under robust stirring for 30 min. The antifouling indexes including the total flux decline ratio (DR
t ), the reversible flux decline ratio (DR
r ), the irreversible flux decline ratio (DR
ir ), and the flux recovery ratio (FRR) were determined as follows:
(6)

R

=

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membranes
ce:section.ce:para: In most cases, TFN membranes comprised of nanoparticles and polymer matrices have higher permeability compared to conventional TFC membranes [23]. Herein, GQDs as a kind of novel carbon material was employed to fabricate the GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membranes. As shown in Fig. 2, the as-prepared GQDs with the size of about 2 nm were stably dispersed in the aqueous solution without obvious aggregation. We compared the GQDs with other common nanoparticles used for fabrication of TFN membranes, as shown in Table 2. Specially, the mixed homogeneous aqueous solutions of GQDs in different concentrations and PIP were easily prepared through simple hand shaking (Fig. S4, Supporting Information).

A series of GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membranes with different GQDs contents were fabricated. The FTIR spectra of the GQDs/PIP-TMC membrane (3#) (Fig. S1, Supporting Information) exhibited the absorption peak at 1639 cm−1 corresponding to the C˭O stretching band of the −CO−NH− groups, which verified the formation of a PA layer on the support membrane [27]. According to the mechanism in Fig. 1, PIP and GQDs in aqueous phase firstly formed a nascent layer on the support membrane. After immersed into organic phase, the amine groups of PIP and the hydroxyl or carboxyl groups of GQDs would react with acyl chloride groups of TMC at oil/water interface zone, which was verified by the FTIR spectra (Fig. S1 & Fig. S2, Supporting Information). Finally, an ultrathin PA layer could be formed upon the porous support.

Fig. 3 showed the XPS measurement results of the PIP-TMC NF membrane (0#) and the GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membrane (1#). The peaks at 284.6, 532.0 and 400.5 eV represented C, O and N elements, respectively. The N/O ratios of the GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membrane (1#) decreased, which showed the changes of cross-linking degree of the PA active layer [28]. Notably, the O/C ratios of the GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membrane (1#) were higher than that of the pristine PIP-TMC NF membrane (0#). This could be due to the oxygen-containing functional groups from GQDs and the changes of cross-linking degree of the PA active layer, indicating the successful incorporation of GQDs. Hu et al. deemed that the acyl chloride groups of TMC could react with the carboxyl or hydroxyl groups in GO to form anhydride or ester bonds [29]. Bano et al. assumed that the carboxyl groups on the edges of GO might react with the secondary amine groups of PIP to form some new amide linkages [13]. Due to the similar features, GQDs might bind with the PA layer through chemical interactions.

The influence of GQDs on the surface morphology of the resultant TFN membranes was characterized by SEM. As can be seen from Fig. 4(a), the PIP-TMC NF membrane (0#) has a dense and rugged surface. This was caused by the fast reaction rate of the interfacial polymerization between PIP and TMC [20]. By contrast, the GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membranes have relatively smooth membrane surfaces. This was because the incorporation of GQDs may bind with PIP or TMC and lower cross-linking reaction rate. It has been proved that lower cross-linking reaction rates and stronger intermolecular hydrogen bonding interactions can result in the formation of smaller nodular structures and smoother membrane surfaces [30]. Moreover, the migration rate of polymer was delayed with the incorporation of GQDs and the microstructures of membranes were changed accordingly. In addition, owing to the small size, GQDs were not observed on the surface of membranes. This indicated good dispersion property of GQDs in the PA layer even at a high GQDs content.

The surface roughness of the GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membranes was further tested by AFM measurement. Fig. 4(b) showed the three-dimensional AFM images of the GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membranes and the values of surface roughness. Consistent with the SEM results, the rough surfaces of the GQDs/PIP-TMC membranes (1#, 3# and 6#) became smooth that the surface roughness was continuously lowered with the increase of GQDs content. The PIP-TMC NF membrane (0#) showed Rms roughness values of 6.09 nm and the value of the GQDs/PIP-TMC membrane (6#) was only 3.86 nm, which was significantly lower than that of the pristine PIP-TMC NF (0#) membrane.

The hydrophilicity of the membrane surfaces was evaluated by the static water contact angle measurement. In general, lower water contact angle represents higher hydrophilicity of surface [31]. As shown in Fig. 5, with the increase of GQDs content from 0 to 0.39 wt%, the water contact angle continuously decreased from 38.0° of the PIP-TMC NF membrane (0#) to 16.0° of the GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membrane (6#), which could be understood from the surface chemical properties and the surface microstructures [32]. On one hand, the introduction of GQDs determined the extent of surface functionalization by carboxyl and hydroxyl groups and the change of surface microstructures [33]; On the other hand, the incorporation of GQDs promoted the formation of water channel structures, which would affect the wettability of membrane surfaces.

Zeta potential of each membrane was tested, as shown in Fig. 6. All membranes were negatively charged mainly due to the deprotonation of carboxyl [34]. It is commonly accepted that the carboxyl groups on membrane surfaces were generated through the hydrolysis of unreacted acyl chloride groups from TMC (–COCl to –COOH). Besides, the GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membrane (1#) exhibited a higher negative charge compared with the pristine PIP-TMC TFC membrane (0#). This was because the carboxyl groups on the edge of GQDs were also introduced to the membrane surface, leading to an increase of the electronegativity of the surface charge. However, it is noteworthy that with the increase of the GQDs content, the surface electronegativity of the GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membranes was gradually decreased. The active functional groups of GQDs, such as hydroxyl groups, would react with TMC during interfacial polymerization and consumed a certain amount of the acyl chloride groups of TMC molecules [13]. Consequently, the amount of the carboxyl groups on the membrane surface was lowered, and the surface charge density of the GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membranes would decrease.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water fluxes and separation performance of the GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membranes
ce:section.ce:para: NF performance of the GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membranes was tested, as shown in Fig. 7. The water flux of the PIP-TMC NF membrane (0#) was only 15.0 L/(m2 h), which was relatively lower because of the dense PA layer. With the GQDs added, the hydrophilicity of the membrane surfaces enhanced. The incorporation of GQDs led to the formation of more water channels within the PA layer, which was beneficial for water to pass through. The water flux of the GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membrane (6#) dramatically increased to 102.0 L/(m2 h), which was 6.8 times higher than that of the pristine PIP-TMC NF (0#) membrane. Separation performance of the GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membranes was summarized in Fig. 7(b), the retention of Congo Red and Orange GII remained above 96.0% and 80.0%, respectively. Based on the results of the PEGs rejections ( Fig. 8 & Table S2), the pore sizes of water channels within the PA layer of the GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membranes increased with the increase of GQDs content. This result is well consistent with the NF performance shown in Fig. 7.

Natural biological membranes are often rendered outstanding transport performance due to the insertion of water channel aquaporins (AQPs) into the matrix of the lipid bilayers [35]. This provides inspiration for the design of synthetic membranes with water channels. In recent years, many kinds of inorganic materials, including carbon nanotubes, zeolite nanoparticles, graphene oxide nanosheets, and metal-organic frameworks with internal and/or external nanochannels were utilized to construct efficient water channels within membranes [3,36]. The hypothesized mechanism of facilitated transport of water through the water channels in the PA layer of the GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membranes was schematically presented in Fig. 9. Owing to the quantum size of GQDs, it could form nanoscale multi-layered water channels at the interface between the GQDs and the PA layer [37]. Moreover, GQDs had smooth and frictionless surface structures, which allowed water molecules to flow through the channels very rapidly [38,39]. The hydrogen bonding interactions between the carboxyl/hydroxyl groups of GQDs and water molecules resulting in additional driving force for water transport within the water channels, which greatly enhanced the water permeability [38]. In summary, the GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membranes have an excellent water flux, which was attributed to the synergistic effect of the surface hydrophilicity and water channels formed at the interface between the GQDs and the PA layer.

In general, the separation mechanism of NF membranes includes the electrostatic repulsive mechanism and steric hindrance [40]. For the rejection of Na2SO4, the mechanism mainly depended on electrostatic repulsive. In Fig. 7(b), the rejection ratio of Na2SO4 was gradually decreased from 90.2% to 40.6%. The results indicated the electrostatic repulsive force between the membrane surface and inorganic salt ions decreased with the decline in negative electronegativity of the membrane surface. This was consistent with the zeta potential results.

Furthermore, we selected the GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membranes (3#, 4#, 5# and 6#) as candidates to compare with TFN NF membranes in literatures. As listed in Table 3, the GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membranes exhibited good NF performance at relatively low pressure. It should be stressed that compared with other state-of-art TFN NF membranes, the GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membranes had much higher water flux with high dye rejections. This was due to the small size and good dispersion of GQDs, which afforded additional water channels within the PA layer without generating non-selective defects.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antifouling performance of the GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membranes
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, antifouling properties of the resultant membranes were assessed through a dead-end filtration experiment using BSA, HA and emulsified oil as model foulants. The time-dependent water fluxes were plotted in Fig. 10. When filtrating the foulant solutions, there was a rapid drop in water fluxes due to the adsorption and deposition of foulants on membrane surfaces. The flux of the GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membrane (3#) declined more sharply than that of the PIP-TMC NF membrane (0#) due to the concentration polarization caused by high flux. After a certain period of operation time, the flux tended to be stable. This could be interpreted by the dynamic equilibrium of the foulants adsorption. Finally, water fluxes of the cleaned membranes (washed with deionized water) were recovered to a relatively high extent.

Specifically, the antifouling indexes were listed in Table 4. It is widely accepted that higher FRR and lower DR
t indicate better antifouling property. The PIP-TMC NF membrane (0#) presented FRR values of 90.32%, 92.79%, and 76.72% for BSA, HA, emulsified oil, respectively, while the GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membrane (3#) showed higher FRR values of 91.85%, 99.06%, and 85.26% for the foulants of BSA, HA, and emulsified oil, respectively. These results indicated that the incorporation of GQDs enhanced the antifouling property of the membrane. The GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membrane (3#) also possessed lower DR
ir values of 8.15%, 0.94%, and 14.74% compared with the PIP-TMC NF membrane (0#). The irreversible membrane fouling was caused by the irreversible foulant adsorption on the membrane surface and pore blockage. The improved antifouling properties of the GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membranes were attributed to the enhanced hydrophilicity and decreased surface roughness, which weakened the interactions between the foulants and membrane surfaces and hence reduced the non-specific adsorption [46].

As can be seen from Table 4, the water fluxes of the GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membrane (3#) under fouling condition with BSA, HA, and emulsified oil solutions were about 4 times as much as those of the pristine PIP-TMC NF membrane (0#). The antifouling membranes with no need for aggressive cleaning procedure often have a longer service life. For the GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membranes, GQDs not only facilitated the water transport through the membrane by creating water channels within the PA layer, but also promoted the formation of the hydration layer on membrane surfaces. The high permeability of the GQDs/PIP-TMC TFN membranes even under harsh fouling conditions showed great potential in practical applications.

In summary, novel TFN membranes have been prepared by interfacial polymerization method with the incorporation of GQDs. Owing to the quantum size and good dispersion, GQDs can efficiently tune the surface roughness, structures and hydrophilicity of the TFN membranes. When the GQDs content varied from 0 to 0.39 wt%, the water flux increased from 15.0 L/m2 h to 102.0 L/m2 h (0.2 MPa), while the rejection to Congo Red was maintained almost unchanged. Additionally, the steady water fluxes of the resultant TFN membranes under harsh fouling conditions were about 4 times as much as those of the pristine PA membrane. The high fluxes of the TFN membranes were attributed to the synergistic effect of the enhancement of the surface hydrophilicity and the formation of water channels within the PA layer. The high NF efficiency, along with the good antifouling properties, eco-friendliness and easy fabrication process made this study very useful for the fabrication of high flux and antifouling membranes in water treatment applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2016YFB0600503).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.memsci.2018.02.010.
